data.headline: Flooring Installation & Refinishing in New Canaan, CT
data.subheadline: Licensed Connecticut flooring contractor serving New Canaan and Fairfield County — hardwood, luxury vinyl plank, tile, and refinishing.

data.title: Professional Flooring in New Canaan, CT
data.content:
New Canaan is nationally recognized for its mid-century modern architectural heritage — the Harvard Five designed iconic homes here in the 1940s and 1950s, and that tradition of thoughtful architecture continues in the town today. The housing stock ranges from those landmark mid-century moderns to traditional colonials to newer contemporary builds. Each requires a different approach to flooring.

BuiltWell serves New Canaan with flooring expertise that respects the architectural context of each home. A mid-century modern with a concrete slab calls for different products and installation methods than a colonial with plank subfloors. We bring the technical knowledge and aesthetic sensitivity that New Canaan homeowners and their architects expect.

data.title: What Is Included in a Flooring Project in New Canaan
data.content:
A complete flooring project in New Canaan covers every phase from preparation through final finish: subfloor assessment and repair, material acclimation, professional installation, transitions, trim, and final cleanup. We handle furniture moving and protection so you don't have to coordinate that separately.

Every project begins with a thorough subfloor assessment — checking for levelness, moisture, squeaks, and structural integrity before any new material goes down. New Canaan homes, particularly those built before 1985, regularly have conditions that need to be addressed before installation to ensure the finished floor performs correctly for years to come.

data.title: What Makes Flooring in New Canaan Different
data.content:
Mid-century modern homes in New Canaan present flooring conditions rarely seen in other Connecticut communities. Polished concrete, cork tile, and original vinyl composite tile are common in these homes. Restoration or replacement of these surfaces requires sourcing compatible materials and understanding the design intent of the original architect.

Contemporary installations in New Canaan often specify large-format tile — 24x24 or larger — in open-plan living spaces, and wide-plank hardwood with low-sheen finishes that read as natural as possible. Execution of these specifications requires precision in subfloor flatness that most standard installations don't demand.

Colonial homes in New Canaan built between 1940 and 1980 have more standard hardwood conditions, though original floors in these homes are often excellent candidates for refinishing rather than replacement.

data.title: New Canaan Building Department and Permits
data.content:
Standard flooring installations in New Canaan do not require building permits. Projects in the historic district or those involving architecturally significant homes may require additional review. Any project involving structural subfloor modification or that is part of a broader permitted renovation will require New Canaan Building Department permits.

We identify these requirements during the consultation. For properties in historically sensitive areas, we flag any additional review timelines during the planning phase.

data.title: Recent Flooring Projects
data.subtitle: See how we've transformed homes across Connecticut with premium flooring installations and refinishing.

items.title: Hardwood Refinishing in Darien
items.before_text: A Darien homeowner uncovered original red oak beneath 40-year-old carpet throughout the main floor. We sanded, applied a custom warm-brown stain, and finished with three coats of satin polyurethane. The floors look brand new.

quote.text: We had no idea these floors were under there. BuiltWell did an incredible job — they look better than anything we could have installed new.
quote.author: The Kellys

items.title: LVP Installation in Stamford
items.before_text: A Stamford condo owner wanted durable, waterproof flooring throughout an open-plan living space over a concrete subfloor. We installed commercial-grade LVP in a wide-plank white oak format with sound-rated underlayment.

quote.text: Looks like hardwood, handles like a tank. I couldn't be happier with how it came out.
quote.author: Marcus T.

items.title: Tile & Hardwood Combination in Westport
items.before_text: A Westport renovation required coordinating large-format porcelain tile in the kitchen and bathrooms with new engineered white oak hardwood throughout the main living areas. We managed both trades and the transitions between them.

quote.text: The transitions between the tile and wood are seamless. The whole first floor looks like one cohesive design.
quote.author: The Rodriguezes

data.title: Why Flooring in New Canaan Requires Local Expertise
data.content:
New Canaan mid-century modern homes on concrete slabs require moisture testing and vapor barrier installation before any hardwood or engineered wood product is installed. Concrete moisture readings above acceptable thresholds eliminate solid hardwood as an option and point toward engineered products or tile. We perform these tests as a standard part of every consultation in these homes.

The open floor plans typical of both mid-century moderns and contemporary New Canaan builds mean large continuous floor areas without the natural break points of room-to-room transitions. Getting the layout, acclimation, and expansion gap management right in these spaces is more complex than in traditional colonial plans.

We bring that experience to every New Canaan project.

data.title: Our Flooring Process
data.subtitle: From consultation to final walkthrough, here's how we deliver exceptional flooring results.

steps.title: Consultation & Assessment
steps.description: We visit your home, assess existing floors, discuss your vision, and measure every room. Moisture testing is included on all lower-level and coastal projects.

steps.title: Material Selection
steps.description: We help you choose from hardwood, LVP, tile, and more based on your lifestyle, subfloor conditions, and budget. We provide clear lead times for every option.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.description: Furniture protection, subfloor inspection and repair, moisture testing, and acclimation of materials on-site. We don't skip steps.

steps.title: Installation
steps.description: Our certified installers work room by room with dust barriers and daily cleanup. You receive daily progress updates throughout.

steps.title: Final Walkthrough
steps.description: We inspect every seam, transition, and finish — you don't pay until you're satisfied. Care and maintenance instructions are provided at closeout.

data.title: Flooring in New Canaan — Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How long does flooring installation take in New Canaan?
items.answer: Installation timelines in New Canaan depend significantly on product. Large-format tile requires careful layout and longer installation time than hardwood. Typical projects run one to two weeks for installation plus any cure or finish time. We provide a complete project schedule with your written proposal so you know exactly what to expect.

items.question: What type of flooring is best for New Canaan homes?
items.answer: Mid-century modern homes in New Canaan are best served by engineered hardwood over concrete or large-format tile with minimal grout lines. Colonial homes are excellent candidates for solid hardwood refinishing or replacement with white oak. LVP is well-suited for lower levels and areas with moisture exposure. The right choice depends on your specific subfloor, moisture conditions, and aesthetic preferences — we assess all of these during the free consultation.

items.question: Do I need permits for flooring work in New Canaan?
items.answer: Standard flooring work in New Canaan does not require permits. Projects in the historic district or involving structural subfloor work require review by the New Canaan Building Department. We identify requirements during the consultation phase.

items.question: How much does flooring cost in New Canaan?
items.answer: New Canaan flooring pricing: hardwood refinishing $4–$7 per sq ft; hardwood installation $10–$18 per sq ft; LVP $7–$14 per sq ft; tile $12–$22 per sq ft. Large-format tile installations in open-plan mid-century homes often run higher due to subfloor flatness requirements. Your written proposal includes a complete cost breakdown by scope so you know exactly where the investment is going.

items.question: Can you refinish existing hardwood floors in New Canaan?
items.answer: Yes for colonial homes with original hardwood. Mid-century modern homes often have different original floor types — concrete, cork, or VCT — which require product-specific assessments. We evaluate every floor individually and provide honest recommendations. Refinishing typically costs $4–$7 per square foot — a fraction of replacement cost — and often produces a result that looks better than new installation.
